title_en: The Sharh on the Hadith of Iman: Comprehensive Faith Theology
title_ar: شرح النووي على حديث الإيمان: عرض شامل لعلم العقيدة
content_en:
The hadith known as the Hadith of Jibril or the Hadith of Iman — in which the Angel Jibril appeared to the Prophet in human form and questioned him about Islam, Iman, and Ihsan — is among the most important narrations in all of hadith literature. Imam Muslim placed it at the very beginning of his Sahih, in the Book of Faith, giving it a prominence that scholars recognized as deliberate. An-Nawawi's commentary on this hadith in the Sharh is one of the most comprehensive treatments of Islamic theology in the commentary tradition, covering a range of questions that define the relationship between faith, works, and salvation.

The hadith's three-part structure — Islam as the five pillars, Iman as the six articles of belief, Ihsan as worshipping God as if you see Him — provided an-Nawawi with a natural framework for organizing a complete theology. He follows this structure carefully, but his treatment goes far beyond simple explanation of each element.

On the question of Iman itself, an-Nawawi engages the long-running scholarly debate about the relationship between belief and action. The Mu'tazilite position that works are part of faith — and that serious sins therefore negate faith — is presented and rejected. The Murji'ite position that works are entirely irrelevant to faith is also addressed and rejected. An-Nawawi presents the Ash'ari view — that faith is affirmation in the heart, verbal declaration, and action of the limbs, but that deficiency in action reduces and increases faith without eliminating it entirely — as the position of Ahl us-Sunnah. He notes that the Shafi'i school and the Hanbali school are broadly in agreement on this definition, though they differ on certain formulations.

The six articles of Iman — belief in God, the angels, the revealed books, the messengers, the Last Day, and divine decree (qadar) — each receive substantial attention. On qadar, an-Nawawi is particularly thorough, since this subject had been the source of major theological controversy from the earliest period of Islamic history. He explains the Ash'ari understanding of how divine decree and human responsibility coexist without denying either, and he addresses the common objections raised by those who felt that a strong doctrine of qadar undermined moral accountability.

The section on Ihsan — the concept of worshipping God with full presence and awareness — opens into a discussion of spiritual excellence that goes beyond standard theological categories. An-Nawawi connects the hadith's definition of Ihsan to the broader tradition of Islamic spirituality, noting that the Sufi tradition and the scholars of the heart (ulama al-batin) have developed this concept extensively, and that their contributions, where grounded in the Quran and Sunnah, are a legitimate part of the Islamic inheritance.

The end of the hadith — in which Jibril tells the Prophet that the questioner was the Angel of Revelation, coming to teach the people their religion — receives an-Nawawi's careful attention. He reads this conclusion as al-Bukhari and Muslim both did: as evidence that Islam, Iman, and Ihsan together constitute a complete definition of the religion, and that any student of Islam should master all three dimensions. The Sharh's commentary on this single hadith functions as an introduction to Islamic theology that students and scholars have used for centuries.
